title: Versals — built-up capital letterforms
chartDefinition: calligraphyExemplar — Roman versals, letters O, A, B, M shown with the three-stroke build-up method
sources: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906) — chapter on built-up capitals and versals
notes: Versals are large decorated letters built from multiple pen strokes rather than written in one move. The two outer strokes define the width; the third stroke fills the counter. The spine of a versal follows the Roman capital proportions. Used as chapter initials, illuminated openers. Johnston's versals are the canonical modern revival form.
